summary: DHH reveals that 61 servers purchased in 2015 for Basecamp 3's launch are only now being retired after nearly a decade of service, at a fully amortized cost of just $142/day. The fleet cost about half a million dollars and supported a service that grossed hundreds of millions. He argues that server lifetimes are typically underestimated—their cloud exit accounting assumed five years, but these lasted nearly ten. The database servers will continue serving heritage apps even after the main fleet is refreshed. DHH uses this as further evidence that owning hardware is vastly more economical than cloud rental, potentially saving millions more than initially projected.
key_points: 61 Dell servers bought for ~$500K in 2015 lasted nearly a decade, working out to just $142/day fully amortized
Server lifetime assumptions in cloud-exit ROI calculations are often too conservative—five years is a floor, not a ceiling
The retiring database servers will be handed down to heritage apps, extracting even more value from the original investment
Hardware ownership combined with lean team management forms the foundation of 37signals' cost advantage
Companies should re-run their cloud-exit calculations with longer server lifetimes, which could tip the economics decisively
money_quotes: That's over 3,500 days of service from this fleet, at a fully amortized cost of just $142/day.
Owning our own iron has been a fantastically profitable proposition. Millions of dollars saved over renting in the cloud.
The dollar you keep at the end of the year is just as real whether you earn it or save it.
Most servers can easily power your applications much longer than that.
So you just might want to run those cloud-exit numbers once more with a longer server lifetime value. It might just tip the equation, and motivate you to become a server owner rather than a renter.
The database servers are getting handed down to some of our heritage apps. They will keep on trucking until they give up the ghost.
key_insight: Server hardware routinely outlasts the conservative five-year depreciation schedules used in cloud-vs-own calculations, making the economics of owning your infrastructure even more favorable than initially projected.
